title: ⚡ Beirut 2020: The 2,750-Ton Blast That Broke a Country
description: On August 4, 2020, at 6:08 PM, approximately 2,750 tons of ammonium nitrate stored in a port warehouse exploded in Beirut, Lebanon. The blast killed 218 people, injured 7,000, and left 300,000 homeless. Lebanese officials had been warned for six years. The investigation has been blocked. The country has not recovered. #Beirut #BeirutExplosion #2020 #AmmoniumNitrate #DisasterDecode

segments.voice: On Tuesday, August 4, 2020, at 6:08 PM Beirut time, approximately 2,750 tons of ammonium nitrate detonated in Warehouse 12 at the Port of Beirut. The blast equivalent to about 1.1 kilotons of TNT made it one of the largest non-nuclear explosions in human history. The shock wave shattered windows across the entire city. The acoustic wave was heard 240 kilometers away in Cyprus. The blast killed 218 people, injured more than 7,000, and left approximately 300,000 people without livable housing. The economic damage exceeded 15 billion dollars. The Lebanese state had been warned for six years that this would happen.
segments.visual: Beirut August 4 2020 6:08 PM Port Warehouse 12 ammonium nitrate blast 218 dead

segments.voice: The ammonium nitrate had arrived in Beirut in 2013. The Russian-flagged ship MV Rhosus, sailing from Georgia toward Mozambique, was diverted to Beirut due to financial difficulties. The vessel was carrying 2,750 tons of ammonium nitrate intended for an industrial buyer in Africa. Lebanese port authorities detained the ship for unpaid port fees and other irregularities. The cargo was eventually transferred to Warehouse 12 in 2014, where it sat unmonitored for the next six years.
segments.visual: MV Rhosus 2013 Russian flag Georgia Mozambique diverted Beirut 2750 tons cargo

segments.voice: Ammonium nitrate is a stable chemical when stored properly. It is the most widely used commercial fertilizer in the world. Approximately 200 million tons are produced annually for legitimate agricultural use. Under proper conditions — dry storage, no contamination, no heat sources — ammonium nitrate is safe for many decades. Under improper conditions — heat, contamination with fuel oil, impact, or fire — it becomes a powerful explosive. The cargo in Warehouse 12 was stored under improper conditions.
segments.visual: ammonium nitrate stable fertilizer 200 million tons proper storage improper explosive

segments.voice: Lebanese officials were aware of the situation. Customs Director Chafic Merhi sent letters to multiple government bodies between 2014 and 2017 warning of the cargo's danger and requesting urgent decisions about its disposal. Letters were sent to the General Directorate of Customs, the State Council, and various ministries. Some recipients responded asking for additional information. Some did not respond at all. None ordered the removal of the cargo. The bureaucratic correspondence accumulated to several hundred pages over six years.
segments.visual: Chafic Merhi customs letters warnings 2014 to 2017 bureaucratic correspondence accumulated

segments.voice: Conditions in Warehouse 12 were known to be poor. The structure had a damaged roof. Doors were left open. Other materials, including fireworks, were stored in the same building. There were no fire suppression systems. There were no temperature controls. There were no security measures preventing unauthorized access. Multiple inspectors visited and noted these problems in writing. The Beirut port management knew. The General Security Directorate knew. The Internal Security Forces knew. The Lebanese Army knew.
segments.visual: Warehouse 12 damaged roof open doors fireworks no suppression no controls inspectors knew

segments.voice: Here's what nobody saw coming on August 4, 2020: a fire. Welders had been working on Warehouse 12's doors that morning. Sparks from the welding ignited stored fireworks. The fireworks burned for several minutes. Witnesses on the streets of Beirut saw smoke rising from the port and began filming on mobile phones. The first explosion occurred at 6:07 PM and was a fireworks detonation — substantial but not catastrophic. The cameras kept rolling. Then, at 6:08 PM, the ammonium nitrate detonated.
segments.visual: August 4 2020 welders fire fireworks ignite 6:07 first explosion 6:08 main detonation

segments.voice: The main blast formed a crater approximately 124 meters wide and 43 meters deep. Where the warehouse had stood, there was now a hole opened to the harbor floor. The shock wave radiated outward at supersonic velocity. Buildings within 1 kilometer of the port were severely damaged. Buildings within 5 kilometers had broken windows. Vehicles and small structures were thrown like leaves. The mushroom cloud from the explosion rose to over 1.5 kilometers in altitude before dispersing.
segments.visual: blast crater 124 meters 43 deep harbor floor shock wave buildings 5 kilometers cloud

segments.voice: Mobile phone videos of the explosion went viral within hours. The footage, captured from multiple angles by people who happened to be filming, became one of the most-viewed video disasters in human history. The wave of compressed air visible in the footage was unmistakable. Buildings can be seen disintegrating in real time. The footage was used by international engineers and physicists in subsequent days to estimate blast yield with unusual precision. The estimate of 1.1 kilotons of TNT was derived directly from video analysis.
segments.visual: mobile phone videos viral compressed air visible buildings disintegrate yield analysis

segments.voice: Hospitals across Beirut were overwhelmed within minutes. The Saint George Hospital in the Achrafieh district was struck directly by the shock wave. Multiple hospital floors collapsed. Four patients in critical care died from the blast itself. Medical staff treated wounded with what medical supplies remained intact. Wounded came on foot, in cars, and in ambulances faster than any single hospital could process. By 7:00 PM, hospitals across the city were turning patients away.
segments.visual: hospitals Saint George Achrafieh shock collapse staff treat wounded 7:00 PM turning away

segments.voice: The 218 deaths were predominantly civilians killed by glass, falling masonry, and structural collapse. The closest casualties were port workers and Beirut Fire Brigade firefighters who had been responding to the warehouse fire. A team of 10 firefighters from the Beirut Fire Brigade died at the scene. Firefighter Sahar Fares had been on the phone with her fiancé when the main explosion occurred. The phone call cut off mid-sentence. Sahar's death became one of the most widely shared individual stories from the disaster.
segments.visual: casualties civilians firefighters Beirut Brigade Sahar Fares phone call individual stories

segments.voice: Approximately 7,000 wounded received medical treatment in the days following. Estimates of additional walking wounded who self-treated reach into the tens of thousands. Many wounded had glass embedded in their bodies — the shards from broken windows had traveled at high velocity. Doctors removed glass from victims for weeks. Several thousand wounded had injuries requiring surgical intervention. Permanent scarring, vision loss, and amputations were common outcomes for severely wounded survivors.
segments.visual: 7000 wounded glass shards velocity surgical intervention scars vision amputations

segments.voice: The destruction of housing was unprecedented in modern Lebanese experience. Approximately 70,000 housing units were damaged. About 6,000 were rendered completely uninhabitable. Approximately 300,000 people were displaced from their homes. The Lebanese government had no functioning emergency housing program. The country's currency had collapsed in the previous year. International humanitarian organizations stepped in to provide tents, food, and medical supplies. Without that aid, the displaced population would have faced winter exposure.
segments.visual: 70000 housing units 6000 uninhabitable 300000 displaced no government international tents

segments.voice: But the tremor was just the start of the political crisis. The Lebanese government, led by Prime Minister Hassan Diab, faced immediate calls to resign. Within six days of the explosion, the entire cabinet had stepped down. Diab himself resigned on August 10. The political vacuum that followed lasted for months. Lebanon had been politically dysfunctional for years before the explosion. The disaster amplified existing dysfunction. New government formation was delayed for over a year.
segments.visual: political crisis Hassan Diab cabinet resignation August 10 vacuum dysfunction year

segments.voice: Lebanon's economic situation in 2020 had been catastrophic before the explosion. The Lebanese pound had lost 80 percent of its value against the dollar over the preceding year. Banks had imposed informal capital controls limiting withdrawals. Inflation was running over 100 percent. Fuel and electricity shortages were daily realities. Unemployment exceeded 30 percent. The middle class had been wiped out. The explosion landed on a country already in collapse and accelerated the collapse.
segments.visual: economic crisis 80 percent currency capital controls inflation 100 percent unemployment 30

segments.voice: Investigation into the explosion was assigned to Judge Tarek Bitar in February 2021. Bitar's investigation has been systematically obstructed since. He summoned political officials, including former ministers, for questioning. Multiple officials refused to appear. Lawsuits were filed against Bitar by his targets, attempting to remove him from the investigation. The Lebanese Constitutional Council issued conflicting rulings. Investigation hearings have been suspended on multiple occasions. By 2024, no senior official has faced trial.
segments.visual: Judge Tarek Bitar investigation 2021 obstruction summons refused lawsuits 2024 no trial

segments.voice: Hezbollah, the Iran-aligned political and militant organization that holds substantial power in Lebanon, has been accused of playing a role in obstructing the investigation. Hezbollah officials have publicly criticized Judge Bitar. The party has called for his removal. Whether Hezbollah had operational knowledge of the ammonium nitrate's storage or use is disputed. Some allegations have suggested the cargo was related to Iranian or Syrian operations; these allegations remain unproven. The political volatility of the case has made full transparency impossible.
segments.visual: Hezbollah obstruction Bitar criticism Iran Syria operations unproven political volatility

segments.voice: International response was substantial in the immediate aftermath. France, the United States, the United Kingdom, and many other countries deployed humanitarian assistance. French President Emmanuel Macron visited Beirut twice in the weeks following the explosion. He pushed for political reform, anti-corruption measures, and the formation of a new government. Macron's interventions were criticized by some as colonial and praised by others as the only effective foreign pressure available. International aid that had been promised was largely conditional on political reforms that did not materialize.
segments.visual: international response France USA Macron Beirut visits reform corruption colonial

segments.voice: The Beirut port was substantially destroyed by the blast. Container terminals, grain silos, and warehousing capacity were damaged or destroyed. The port had been Lebanon's main commercial gateway. Importers shifted to alternative ports in Tripoli and Sidon. The reconstruction of the Beirut port has been delayed by political disputes about who would lead it. By 2024, the port has not been fully reconstructed. Lebanon's commercial supply chain has been distorted for years as a consequence.
segments.visual: Beirut port destroyed terminals silos Tripoli Sidon reconstruction delayed supply chain

segments.voice: Comparable ammonium nitrate explosions have occurred elsewhere. The 1947 Texas City disaster involved approximately 2,300 tons of ammonium nitrate exploding aboard the SS Grandcamp; 581 people died. The 2001 AZF explosion in Toulouse, France, involved approximately 300 tons; 31 died. The 2013 West, Texas explosion involved 30 tons; 15 died. Beirut sits at the higher end of this scale historically. The combination of bulk storage, urban setting, and uncontained warehouse made it particularly devastating.
segments.visual: comparable disasters Texas City 1947 AZF Toulouse 2001 West Texas 2013 Beirut scale

segments.voice: International ammonium nitrate handling regulations have been substantially tightened since multiple major disasters. The European Union issued the Seveso III Directive requiring detailed risk assessment for chemical storage facilities. The United States Occupational Safety and Health Administration issued enhanced regulations after West, Texas. The International Maritime Organization has issued port handling guidance. Lebanon's regulatory framework had not been updated to comply with international best practice before 2020.
segments.visual: ammonium nitrate regulations EU Seveso OSHA West Texas IMO Lebanon outdated

segments.voice: Reconstruction of damaged Beirut housing has been slow. The Beirut Heritage initiative documented and protected hundreds of historic buildings damaged by the blast. International funds were promised for restoration. Implementation has been hampered by Lebanon's economic collapse and political dysfunction. Many damaged buildings remain partially repaired or abandoned years after the explosion. Tourism, formerly an important Lebanese industry, has been depressed by the visible damage and ongoing political instability.
segments.visual: Beirut Heritage initiative historic buildings funds promised reconstruction slow tourism

segments.voice: The mental health impact of the explosion has been substantial and persistent. Beirut residents who lived through the blast describe acute trauma symptoms — flashbacks, hypervigilance, sleep disorders. The Lebanese mental health system, already underfunded before 2020, has been overwhelmed by demand. International humanitarian organizations have provided support, but coverage is uneven. Many residents have left the country in the years since, contributing to a substantial Lebanese diaspora wave.
segments.visual: mental health trauma flashbacks hypervigilance system overwhelmed diaspora wave

segments.voice: Lebanese activist Sahar Mandour and many others have organized memorial events on the anniversary of the explosion each year. The events demand justice, accountability, and political reform. The names of the 218 dead are read aloud. Photographs of the destroyed city are displayed. The anniversary has become a national focal point for ongoing demands that the Lebanese state never fully accept. The unanswered questions of the disaster remain a wound on Lebanese politics.
segments.visual: Sahar Mandour memorial activist anniversary names photographs national focal wound

segments.voice: What does Beirut teach? Bureaucratic systems can fail catastrophically. Authorities can know about a hazard for years, document it, send warnings, and still take no action. The chain of paper from 2014 to 2020 includes everything a functioning state would need to prevent the disaster. The disaster occurred not because the warnings were absent but because the political system could not act on what its own bureaucracy had identified. Lebanese collapse was visible in the failure to remove a known bomb from a populated port.
segments.visual: Beirut lesson bureaucratic failure chain paper warnings inaction collapse visible bomb

segments.voice: Subscribe to DisasterDecode and hit the bell. Next week's disaster goes deeper into the Sahel droughts of the 1970s and 1980s — the climate disaster that killed at least 100,000 people across West Africa and reshaped global understanding of desertification.
segments.visual: Sahel droughts 1970s 1980s West Africa 100000 desertification channel
